title: Massive Lava Tube Discovered on Venus—Bigger Than Any on Earth
summary:
Venus, Earth's so-called "sister planet," has just given up a secret it's been hiding for billions of years: a massive underground lava tube.

Scientists discovered it by taking a fresh look at radar images captured by NASA's Magellan spacecraft in the early 1990s. Using advanced imaging techniques, they spotted a collapsed "skylight" near a volcano called Nyx Mons. Further analysis revealed that this skylight opens into an empty lava tube at least 375 meters (1,230 feet) deep. The researchers estimate the tube could be up to one kilometer (0.6 mile) wide—larger than any lava tube found on Earth or Mars.

"It is remarkable that we are still extracting new insights from Magellan data," says Anna Gülcher, a planetary scientist at the University of Freiburg who wasn't involved in the study. She points to "the lasting value of that mission, the progress we have made in data analysis and the renewed interest in the planet."

Lava tubes form when molten rock flows beneath the surface and then drains away, leaving a hollow tunnel. On the Moon and Mars, scientists have long considered using such tubes as shelters for astronauts, protecting them from solar radiation and meteorites. But Venus is a different story. The surface pressure is 93 times that of Earth, and temperatures are hot enough to melt regular electronics. So human exploration of this particular tube is out of the question.

Still, the discovery raises intriguing questions. Venus has gravity similar to Earth's, so why is this tube so much larger than terrestrial ones? Lorenzo Bruzzone, a remote-sensing scientist at the University of Trento and co-author of the study published in Nature Communications, says finding one lava tube suggests there could be more.

The discovery comes at a pivotal time. Two upcoming missions—NASA's VERITAS (before June 2031) and the European Space Agency's EnVision (later that year)—will carry advanced radar instruments capable of imaging Venus's surface at significantly higher resolution. They may reveal even more hidden features beneath the planet's thick clouds.
why_it_matters: Understanding Venus’s geology helps scientists learn how Earth-like planets evolve, and the discovery of a giant lava tube raises questions about volcanic processes that could apply to other planets — knowledge that deepens our understanding of the solar system and our own planet’s history.

keywords.explanation: A hollow tunnel left behind when molten rock drains away underground.
A collapsed hole in the roof of a lava tube that lets scientists see inside from above.
A NASA space probe that mapped Venus using radar in the early 1990s.
High-energy particles and rays from the Sun that can harm humans and electronics.
Rocks from space that survive traveling through the atmosphere and hit the ground.
A planned NASA mission to Venus that will use advanced radar to map the planet in high detail.

By sending a rover to Venus

questions.question: Which upcoming missions will study Venus with better radar?

background_read: Venus is often called Earth's 'sister planet' because it is similar in size and gravity, but its surface is extremely hostile: the pressure is 93 times Earth's (equivalent to being 900 meters underwater) and temperatures hover around 460°C (860°F), hot enough to melt lead.

The Magellan spacecraft (NASA, 1990-1994) used radar to pierce Venus's thick clouds and map nearly the entire surface. Despite its age, the data still yields new discoveries when re-analyzed with modern techniques.

Lava tubes have been found on the Moon and Mars, where they are considered potential natural shelters for astronauts, protecting them from cosmic rays and micrometeorite impacts. However, Venus's extreme conditions make any human use of this tube impossible for now.

The upcoming VERITAS mission (NASA, launching before June 2031) and EnVision mission (European Space Agency, launching later in 2031) will carry advanced radar that can see details as small as 30 meters across, compared to Magellan's 120-meter resolution. They may reveal many more hidden features beneath Venus's clouds.
Article_Structure: LEAD: Venus has given up a secret: a massive underground lava tube.
└─ SOURCE: New analysis of 1990s Magellan radar images
DISCOVERY: A collapsed 'skylight' near volcano Nyx Mons, opening into a tube ≥375 m deep
└─ SIZE: Up to 1 km wide — bigger than any found on Earth or Mars
CONTEXT: Lava tubes form when molten rock drains; on Moon/Mars they could be shelters
└─ BUT: Venus's surface pressure (93x Earth) and heat (460°C) make exploration impossible
SCIENTIFIC PUZZLE: Why so large, given Venus's Earth-like gravity?
└─ QUOTE: 'remarkable that we are still extracting new insights from Magellan data' — Anna Gülcher
FUTURE: VERITAS (NASA) and EnVision (ESA) will map Venus with much higher resolution radar

perspectives.description: The discovery provides a rare glimpse into Venus’s subsurface geology. It proves that old data can still yield new breakthroughs when combined with modern analysis techniques, and it sets a target for future high-resolution radar missions.

Unlike lava tubes on the Moon or Mars, this Venus tube offers no immediate benefit for human exploration. The planet’s extreme environment means the tube will remain strictly a scientific curiosity, not a potential habitat or resource site.

The finding highlights the lasting value of planetary missions that end decades ago. Magellan cost about $800 million (in 1990s dollars) and has now produced useful science for over 30 years. This success suggests that archiving and re-analyzing old data is a cost-effective strategy.

With VERITAS and EnVision scheduled before 2032, scientists expect a flood of new discoveries about Venus. If more lava tubes are found, they could reveal clues about Venus’s past — perhaps even evidence that the planet once had water or volcanic activity that reshaped its surface.
